sentence.target_text: She says hello to her teacher.

gloss.text: SECTION 1 · FOUNDATIONS
First Words

Welcome to your very first English unit! In these lessons you will learn simple greetings like 'hello', 'good morning', and 'goodbye', and you will introduce yourself with 'I am' and 'you are'. You will also practice polite words such as 'please' and 'thank you', and you will ask 'What is your name?'. Tip: say each new phrase out loud two or three times - speaking early makes the words easy to remember. Enjoy your first words in English!
How do you greet someone in the morning?
'Good morning' is the standard greeting from early morning until about midday.
Correct. 'Good morning' is the greeting people use in the morning.
'Good night' is for leaving or bedtime, not for greeting someone.
'Goodbye' is for leaving, not for greeting someone.
Which word do you say when you leave?
'Goodbye' is the standard word to say when you leave.
Correct. 'Goodbye' is the word people say when they leave.
'Hello' is for meeting someone, not for leaving.
'Hi' is an informal greeting for meeting someone, not for leaving.
'Good morning' greets someone at the start of the day; it is not for leaving.
It is late and you are going to bed. What do you say?
'Good night' is what you say before bed or when you leave at night.
Correct. 'Good night' is for bedtime or for leaving at night.
'Good morning' is for the start of the day, not for bedtime.
'Hi' is a greeting for meeting someone, not for bedtime.
Which word is an informal way to say 'hello'?
'Hi' is a short, friendly, informal version of 'hello'.
Correct. 'Hi' is a short, informal way to say 'hello'.
'Goodbye' is for leaving; it is not a way to say 'hello'.
'Good night' is for bedtime or leaving at night, not a greeting.
You meet your teacher at eight in the morning. Write the greeting in English.
'Good morning' is the greeting used in the morning.
You see your friend. Say hello and call him your friend. Write the full sentence in English.
You can greet a friend with 'Hello' and add 'my friend' to be warm and friendly.
'Good night' is said at bedtime or when someone leaves at night.
Complete the sentence: I ___ Anna.
The verb 'be' with 'I' is 'am': 'I am Anna.'
Correct. With 'I', the verb 'be' is 'am': 'I am Anna.'
'Are' goes with 'you', not with 'I'. With 'I', use 'am'.
'Is' goes with 'he', 'she', or 'it', not with 'I'. With 'I', use 'am'.
Complete the sentence: You ___ my friend.
The verb 'be' with 'you' is 'are': 'You are my friend.'
Correct. With 'you', the verb 'be' is 'are': 'You are my friend.'
'Am' goes with 'I', not with 'you'. With 'you', use 'are'.
'Is' goes with 'he', 'she', or 'it', not with 'you'. With 'you', use 'are'.
Which sentence is correct?
Use 'am' with 'I' and 'are' with 'you'.
Correct. 'I' takes 'am', so 'I am Anna.' is right.
'Are' does not go with 'I'. Use 'am': 'I am Anna.'
'Am' does not go with 'you'. Use 'are': 'You are my friend.'
Introduce yourself as Anna. Write the full sentence in English.
To introduce yourself, use 'I am' plus your name: 'I am Anna.'
Tell a person that they are your friend. Write the full sentence in English.
With 'you', use 'are': 'You are my friend.'
'I am your friend.' uses 'am' because the subject is 'I'.
A person gives you a gift. What do you say?
Say 'Thank you' to show thanks when someone gives you something or helps you.
Correct. 'Thank you' shows thanks when you receive something.
'You are welcome' is the reply to 'thank you', not what you say when you receive a gift.
'Please' makes a request polite; it does not show thanks.
A person says 'Thank you.' What is the polite reply?
The polite reply to 'thank you' is 'You are welcome.'
Correct. 'You are welcome' is the standard reply to 'thank you'.
'Please' makes a request polite; it is not a reply to 'thank you'.
'Excuse me' gets a person's attention; it is not a reply to 'thank you'.
'Good night' is for leaving at night or bedtime, not a reply to 'thank you'.
You want to pass a person in a crowded place. What do you say?
'Excuse me' is the polite phrase to get attention or ask a person to let you pass.
Correct. 'Excuse me' politely asks a person to let you pass.
'Thank you' shows thanks; it does not ask a person to let you pass.
'You are welcome' is a reply to 'thank you', not a way to ask for space.
Which word makes a request polite? 'Water, ___.'
Add 'please' to a request to make it polite: 'Water, please.'
Correct. 'Please' makes a request polite: 'Water, please.'
'Goodbye' is for leaving; it does not make a request polite.
'Hello' is a greeting; it does not make a request polite.
A person helps you. Show your thanks. Write it in English.
'Thank you' is the standard way to show thanks in English.
Reply politely to 'Thank you.' Write the reply in English.
'You are welcome' is the polite reply to 'thank you'.
'Yes, please.' is the polite way to say yes to an offer.
You want to know a person's name. What do you ask?
'What is your name?' is the question that asks for a person's name.
Correct. 'What is your name?' asks for a person's name.
'How are you?' asks how a person feels, not their name.
'Nice to meet you.' is a friendly phrase after you learn a name; it is not a question.
Complete the answer: '___ name is Anna.'
Answer with 'My name is' plus your name: 'My name is Anna.'
Correct. Use 'my' before 'name' to talk about your own name.
'You' is a subject pronoun; before 'name' you need 'my'.
'I' is the subject in 'I am Anna.'; before 'name', use 'my'.
You meet a new person and learn their name. What do you say?
Say 'Nice to meet you.' when you meet a person for the first time.
Correct. 'Nice to meet you.' is the friendly phrase for meeting someone new.
'Good night.' is for bedtime or leaving at night, not for meeting someone.
'You are welcome.' is a reply to 'thank you', not for meeting someone.
Put the words in the correct order: is / name / My / Anna
The correct order is 'My name is Anna.'
Correct. The order is 'My' + 'name' + 'is' + the name.
'Is' must come after 'name': 'My name is Anna.'
'My' comes before 'name': 'My name is Anna.'
Ask for a person's name in English.
'What is your name?' (or 'What's your name?') asks for a person's name.
Say that your name is Anna. Write the full sentence in English.
Use 'My name is' plus your name: 'My name is Anna.'
'Nice to meet you.' is the friendly phrase for meeting someone new.
Anna's First Day
This story practices the greetings and introductions from Unit 1: saying hello, good morning, and goodbye at school.
'Anna is a new student' — we use 'is' with one person (he, she, Anna).
'Says hello' — 'hello' works at any time of day.
'Good morning' is the greeting before midday.
'Asks her name' — to ask a name, say: What is your name?
'Goodbye' is for leaving — the opposite of 'hello'.
Morning Greetings — Episode 1
A short listening episode: two people greet each other and introduce themselves.
Saying Hello at School
Meet a New Classmate
Greet Ben and introduce yourself politely.
Full marks: greet Ben, reply politely, and use a phrase from this unit.
Nice to meet you.
Good night.
Goodbye.
Ben says: 'Hello, my name is Ben.' Choose the polite reply.
'Nice to meet you' is the polite reply when someone tells you their name.
Correct — 'Nice to meet you' answers an introduction politely.
'Good night' is for bedtime or leaving at night, not for meeting someone.
'Goodbye' ends a conversation — Ben has just introduced himself.
First Day Adventure
Talk your way through your first morning at school.
Say good morning to the teacher
Say hello to the new boy
Introduce yourself in two short sentences. Use 'hello' and your name.
Write at least 6 words. Say hello and give your name. End sentences with a full stop.
A good answer greets and gives a name, e.g.: 'Hello! My name is Anna.'
Who is the new student in the story?
The first line says: 'Anna is a new student.'
Correct — the story starts: 'Anna is a new student.'
Ben is the boy Anna meets, not the new student in the story.
The teacher greets Anna — the teacher is not new.
What does the teacher say to Anna?
The teacher greets her in the morning: 'Good morning.'
Correct — the story says the teacher says good morning.
'Good night' is for the evening — the story happens in the morning.
'Thank you' shows thanks — the teacher is greeting, not thanking.
About You

Welcome to 'About You'! In this unit, you learn to say where you are from, like 'I am from Japan.' You also practice 'he is' and 'she is' to talk about other people. Finally, you count from one to ten and give your age: 'I am ten years old.'
Choose the correct word: I am ___ Japan.
Which sentence tells someone where you are from?
Maria asks: 'Where are you from?' Choose the best answer.
Choose the country name to complete the sentence: I am from ___.
Tell someone you are from Japan. Write the sentence.
Ask someone where they are from. Write the question.
Tap what you hear.
Choose the correct word: He ___ my friend.
Choose the correct word: She ___ from France.
Which sentence is correct?
Anna is a woman. Choose the correct word: ___ is from Brazil.
Say that he is from India. Write the sentence.
Say that she is your friend. Write the sentence.
Tap what you hear.
Choose the word for the number 2.
Choose the word for the number 5.
What number comes after seven?
Choose the word for the number 10.
Write the number 3 as a word.
Write the number 7 as a word.
Tap what you hear.
Choose the correct word: I am ten years ___.
Tom asks: 'How old are you?' Choose the best answer.
Choose the correct question to ask about age.
Choose the correct word: She is nine ___ old.
Say that you are ten years old. Write the sentence.
Ask someone how old they are. Write the question.
Tap what you hear.
We use 'from' plus a country to say where we come from: 'I am from Japan.'
'I am from France.' follows the pattern 'I am from' plus a country, which tells people where you come from.
'Where are you from?' asks about your country, so 'I am from Brazil.' is the answer that fits.
Only a country name can complete 'I am from ___', and 'India' is a country.
'I am from Japan.' uses the pattern 'I am from' plus a country name.
'Where are you from?' is the question we use to ask about someone's country.
The audio says 'I am from Mexico.' The speaker is telling you their country.
With 'he' and 'she', the verb 'be' becomes 'is': 'He is my friend.'
'She' takes 'is', so the correct sentence is 'She is from France.'
'He' always goes with 'is', so 'He is my friend.' is the only correct sentence.
Anna is a woman, so we talk about her with 'she': 'She is from Brazil.'
With 'he', we use 'is': 'He is from India.'
With 'she', we use 'is': 'She is my friend.'
The audio says 'She is Anna.' We use 'is' with 'she' to say who someone is.
The word for the number 2 is 'two'.
The word for the number 5 is 'five'.
When we count from one to ten, 'eight' comes right after 'seven'.
The word for the number 10 is 'ten'.
The number 3 is written as the word 'three'.
The number 7 is written as the word 'seven'.
The audio counts 'one, two, three', the first three numbers.
We give our age with the pattern 'I am ... years old.'
'How old are you?' asks about age, so the answer gives a number of years: 'I am eight years old.'
'How old are you?' is the question we use to ask about someone's age.
After a number bigger than one, we use the plural 'years': 'She is nine years old.'
'I am ten years old.' follows the age pattern 'I am ... years old.'
'How old are you?' is how we ask someone's age.
The audio says 'ten years old', the phrase we use to give an age.
'Is' does not fit here. It is the form of 'be' we use with 'he' or 'she', and this sentence already has 'am'.
Correct. 'From' plus a country tells people where you come from: 'I am from Japan.'
'Old' does not fit here. It describes age, as in 'ten years old', not the country you come from.
Correct. 'I am from France.' uses 'I am from' plus a country to say where you come from.
'Thank you.' does not tell where you are from. It is a polite phrase you say when someone helps you.
'Good morning.' does not tell where you are from. It is a greeting you use early in the day.
'Good night.' does not answer the question. You say it in the evening when you leave or go to bed.
'You are welcome.' does not answer the question. It is the polite reply to 'Thank you.'
Correct. 'I am from Brazil.' names a country, so it answers 'Where are you from?'
'Old' is not a country. It describes age, as in 'ten years old'.
Correct. 'India' is a country name, so 'I am from India.' makes sense.
'Am' is not a country. It is a form of 'be' and is already in the sentence.
'Am' does not go with 'he'. We use 'am' only with 'I', as in 'I am'.
'Are' does not go with 'he'. We use 'are' with 'you', as in 'you are'.
Correct. With 'he' and 'she', the verb 'be' becomes 'is': 'He is my friend.'
Correct. With 'she', we use 'is': 'She is from France.'
'Are' does not go with 'she'. We use 'are' with 'you', as in 'Where are you from?'
'Am' does not go with 'she'. We use 'am' only with 'I', as in 'I am from France.'
This sentence is not correct. 'Are' goes with 'you', not with 'he'.
Correct. 'He' takes 'is', so 'He is my friend.' is the right sentence.
This sentence is not correct. 'Am' goes only with 'I', not with 'he'.
Correct. Anna is a woman, so we use 'she': 'She is from Brazil.'
'He' is for a man or a boy. Anna is a woman, so 'he' does not fit.
'Am' is a form of 'be', not a word for a person. The sentence needs 'she'.
'Ten' is the word for the number 10, not 2.
Correct. 'Two' is the word for the number 2.
'Three' is the word for the number 3, not 2.
Correct. 'Five' is the word for the number 5.
'Four' is the word for the number 4, not 5.
'Nine' is the word for the number 9, not 5.
'Six' comes before 'seven' when we count, not after it.
'Ten' comes later. It is the last number when we count from one to ten.
Correct. 'Eight' comes right after 'seven': seven, eight, nine.
'One' is the word for the number 1, not 10.
Correct. 'Ten' is the word for the number 10.
'Seven' is the word for the number 7, not 10.
Correct. Age ends with 'old': 'I am ten years old.'
'From' does not fit here. It tells where you come from, as in 'I am from Japan.'
'Name' does not fit here. It is what people call you, as in 'My name is Anna.'
'I am from Japan.' answers 'Where are you from?', not a question about age.
'Nice to meet you.' does not give an age. You say it when you meet someone new.
Correct. 'I am eight years old.' gives an age, so it answers 'How old are you?'
'Where are you from?' asks about a person's country, not their age.
Correct. 'How old are you?' asks how many years old someone is.
'What is your name?' asks what people call you, not their age.
'Year' is singular. After the number nine, we need the plural 'years'.
'From' does not fit here. It tells where someone comes from, not their age.
Correct. After numbers bigger than one, we use the plural: 'nine years old'.
Family & Friends

In this unit, you meet the family: mother, father, brother, and sister. You learn to introduce people with 'This is my...' and to ask 'Who is this?' when you meet someone new. You also use 'we are' and 'they are' to talk about more than one person. Soon you can say 'We are friends!' with confidence.
Choose the word for your female parent.
'Mother' is the word for a female parent. 'Father' and 'brother' name male family members.
'Brother' is not a parent. A brother is a boy or man with the same parents as you.
Correct. Your mother is your female parent.
'Father' is a parent, but a male one, not a female one.
Choose the word for your male parent.
'Father' means a male parent, so it is the right choice.
Correct. Your father is your male parent.
'Mother' means a female parent, not a male parent.
'Sister' is not a parent. A sister is a girl or woman with the same parents as you.
Complete the sentence: 'My ___ is a boy.'
A brother is a boy, so 'brother' completes the sentence correctly.
'Sister' means a girl, so it does not fit 'is a boy.'
'Mother' means a woman, your female parent, so she is not a boy.
Correct. A brother is a boy, so 'My brother is a boy.' makes sense.
Complete the sentence: 'My ___ is a girl.'
A sister is a girl, so 'sister' completes the sentence correctly.
'Father' means a man, your male parent, so he is not a girl.
Correct. A sister is a girl, so 'My sister is a girl.' makes sense.
'Brother' means a boy, so it does not fit 'is a girl.'
Look at a photo of your mother. Say who she is, starting with 'She'. Write the sentence.
Use 'my' before a family word to show she belongs to your family: 'She is my mother.'
Tell a friend about your brother. Say who he is, starting with 'He'. Write the sentence.
'He is my brother.' uses 'my' plus a family word. 'My' shows the person is in your family.
Tap what you hear.
The audio says 'my family.' 'My' plus 'family' talks about the people in your home.
Your sister stands next to you. Complete the introduction: '___ is my sister.'
Use 'This is my...' to introduce one person who is near you.
Correct. 'This' points to one person who is close to you.
'These' points to more than one person or thing, so it does not fit with 'is.'
'Am' is the form of 'be' used with 'I,' not a word for pointing to a person.
Your father is across the street. Complete the sentence: '___ is my father.'
Use 'That is...' for one person who is far away, like across the street.
'Those' points to more than one person or thing, so it does not fit with 'is.'
'Are' is a form of the verb 'be,' not a word for pointing to a person.
Correct. 'That' points to one person who is far from you.
Complete the introduction: 'This ___ my friend Anna.'
After 'this,' use 'is': 'This is my friend Anna.'
'Are' goes with 'we,' 'you,' and 'they,' not with 'this.'
Correct. 'This' takes 'is': 'This is my friend Anna.'
'Am' goes only with 'I,' as in 'I am.'
You introduce your grandmother. Choose the correct sentence.
The introduction pattern is 'This is my' plus a person: 'This is my grandmother.'
Correct. 'This is my...' is the pattern for introducing one person.
The word order is wrong. The verb 'is' comes right after 'this.'
'Are' does not go with 'this.' Use 'is' for one person.
Introduce your brother to a friend. Write the sentence.
To introduce a person near you, use 'This is my...': 'This is my brother.'
Your sister is across the room. Introduce her with 'That is'. Write the sentence.
Use 'That is my...' for a person who is not close to you: 'That is my sister.'
Tap what you hear.
The audio says 'this is my friend,' the pattern for introducing a person near you.
Complete the question about a person: '___ is this?'
'Who' is the question word for people, so 'Who is this?' asks about a person.
'What' asks about things, not people. 'What is this?' asks about an object.
Correct. 'Who' asks about a person: 'Who is this?'
'Yes' is an answer word, not a question word.
Choose the correct answer to the question: 'Who is this?'
'Who is this?' asks about a person, so a good answer names the person: 'This is my mother.'
'Yes, it is.' answers a yes/no question, but 'Who is this?' asks for a person.
'I am fine.' answers 'How are you?', not 'Who is this?'
Correct. The question asks about a person, and this answer names the person.
Choose the question with the correct word order.
In who-questions, 'who' comes first, then 'is': 'Who is that?'
Correct. The question word comes first, then the verb: 'Who is that?'
The question word 'who' must come first, not the verb: 'Who is that?'
The order is wrong. Start with 'who,' then 'is,' then 'that.'
Complete the question: 'Who ___ they?'
'They' always takes 'are,' so the question is 'Who are they?'
'Is' goes with one person, like 'he' or 'she,' not with 'they.'
'Am' goes only with 'I,' as in 'I am.'
Correct. 'They' takes 'are': 'Who are they?'
Ask about the person next to you. Write the question with 'who' and 'this'.
To ask about a person near you, say 'Who is this?' 'Who' asks for the person's identity.
You see a man far away. Ask who he is, using 'that'. Write the question.
Use 'that' for a person far away: 'Who is that?'
Tap what you hear.
The audio says 'who is this,' the question that asks about a person near you.
Complete the sentence: 'We ___ friends.'
'We' always takes 'are,' so the sentence is 'We are friends.'
'Is' goes with one person or thing, like 'he' or 'she,' not with 'we.'
Correct. 'We' takes 'are': 'We are friends.'
'Am' goes only with 'I,' as in 'I am.'
Complete the sentence: 'They ___ my sisters.'
'They' is plural and takes 'are': 'They are my sisters.'
Correct. 'They' takes 'are': 'They are my sisters.'
'Is' goes with one person, but 'they' means more than one.
'Am' goes only with 'I,' not with 'they.'
Choose the correct sentence.
With 'we,' the verb 'be' becomes 'are': 'We are a family.'
'Is' does not go with 'we.' Use 'are' with 'we.'
'Am' goes only with 'I,' not with 'we.'
Correct. 'We' takes 'are': 'We are a family.'
You point at two boys. Complete the sentence: '___ are my brothers.'
Two boys are more than one person, so use 'They' with 'are.'
'He' means one boy or man and takes 'is,' not 'are.'
Correct. 'They' means more than one person and takes 'are.'
'I' means yourself and takes 'am,' not 'are.'
Say that you and your friend are friends. Write the sentence with 'we'.
'We' includes you and another person and takes 'are': 'We are friends.'
Anna and Ben are your friends. Say that they are your friends. Write the sentence.
'They' talks about two or more people and takes 'are': 'They are my friends.'
Tap what you hear.
The audio says 'we are friends.' 'We' takes the plural verb 'are.'
Everyday Things

Welcome to Everyday Things! In this unit, you learn to name the objects around you with 'a' and 'an,' like 'a pen' and 'an apple.' You practice simple sentences with 'it is,' and you add colors like red, blue, and green. At the end, you ask and answer the question 'What is this?'
Which phrase is correct?
'A book' is correct because 'book' starts with a consonant sound. We use 'a' before consonant sounds and 'an' before vowel sounds.
Correct. 'Book' starts with a consonant sound, so it takes 'a': a book.
Incorrect. 'An' goes before vowel sounds, like 'an apple.' 'Book' starts with a consonant sound, so we say 'a book.'
Incorrect. 'Apple' starts with a vowel sound, so we say 'an apple,' not 'a apple.'
Complete the sentence: It is ___ apple.
'An' is correct because 'apple' starts with a vowel sound: It is an apple.
Incorrect. 'A' goes before consonant sounds, like 'a pen.' 'Apple' starts with a vowel sound, so we use 'an.'
Correct. 'Apple' starts with a vowel sound, so we use 'an': It is an apple.
Incorrect. 'And' joins words, like 'a pen and a book.' It is not an article.
Complete the sentence: It is ___ pen.
'A' is correct because 'pen' starts with a consonant sound: It is a pen.
Incorrect. 'An' goes before vowel sounds, like 'an egg.' 'Pen' starts with a consonant sound, so we use 'a.'
Incorrect. 'And' connects two things, like 'an apple and an egg.' The sentence needs the article 'a.'
Correct. 'Pen' starts with a consonant sound, so we use 'a': It is a pen.
Which phrase is correct?
'An umbrella' is correct because 'umbrella' starts with a vowel sound, so it takes 'an.'
Incorrect. 'Umbrella' starts with a vowel sound, so we say 'an umbrella,' not 'a umbrella.'
Correct. 'Umbrella' starts with a vowel sound, so it takes 'an': an umbrella.
Incorrect. 'Chair' starts with a consonant sound, so we say 'a chair,' not 'an chair.'
You point at an egg. Say what it is. Write the sentence.
'It is an egg.' names one object. 'Egg' starts with a vowel sound, so we use 'an.'
You point at a chair. Say what it is. Write the sentence.
'It is a chair.' names one object. 'Chair' starts with a consonant sound, so we use 'a.'
Tap what you hear.
The audio says 'an umbrella.' 'Umbrella' starts with a vowel sound, so it takes 'an.'
Choose the correct sentence.
'It is a book.' is correct: the subject 'it,' the verb 'is,' and the article 'a' before 'book.'
Incorrect. This sentence is missing the verb 'is.' We say 'It is a book.'
Incorrect. This sentence is missing the article 'a.' We say 'It is a book.'
Correct. 'It is a book.' has the subject 'it,' the verb 'is,' and the article 'a' before 'book.'
Complete the sentence: It ___ a pen.
With 'it,' the verb is 'is': It is a pen.
Correct. With 'it,' we use 'is': It is a pen.
Incorrect. 'Am' goes with 'I,' as in 'I am Anna.' With 'it,' we use 'is.'
Incorrect. 'Are' goes with 'you,' 'we,' or 'they.' With 'it,' we use 'is.'
Choose the correct sentence.
'It is an umbrella.' is correct because 'umbrella' starts with a vowel sound, so it takes 'an.'
Incorrect. 'Umbrella' starts with a vowel sound, so we use 'an,' not 'a.'
Correct. 'It is' names the object, and 'an' goes before the vowel sound in 'umbrella.'
Incorrect. This sentence is missing an article. We say 'It is an umbrella.'
Complete the sentence: ___ is a bag.
'It' is the subject of the sentence: It is a bag.
Correct. 'It' is the subject: It is a bag.
Incorrect. 'Is' is the verb, and the sentence already has 'is.' It needs the subject 'it' first.
Incorrect. 'An' is an article for vowel sounds, like 'an egg.' The sentence needs the subject 'it.'
You point at a book. Say what it is. Write the sentence.
'It is a book.' uses 'it is' to name the object you point at.
You point at an apple. Say what it is. Write the sentence.
'It is an apple.' uses 'it is' plus 'an' before the vowel sound in 'apple.'
Tap what you hear.
The audio says 'it is a pen,' the sentence we use to name a pen.
Which word is a color?
'Red' is a color word. 'Chair' and 'egg' are objects, not colors.
Incorrect. A chair is an object you sit on, not a color.
Correct. 'Red' is a color, like the color of many apples.
Incorrect. An egg is a food, not a color.
Choose the correct sentence.
'It is a red book.' is correct because the color 'red' goes after 'a' and before 'book.'
Incorrect. The color goes before the object: 'a red book,' not 'a book red.'
Incorrect. This sentence is missing the article 'a.' We say 'It is a red book.'
Correct. The article 'a' and the color 'red' go before 'book': It is a red book.
Grass is this color. Choose the color.
Grass is green, so 'green' is the correct color.
Correct. Grass is green.
Incorrect. 'Black' is a very dark color, like the night sky. Grass is green.
Incorrect. 'White' is a very light color, like snow. Grass is green.
Snow is this color. Choose the color.
Snow is white, so 'white' is the correct color.
Incorrect. 'Black' is a very dark color, like the night sky. Snow is white.
Incorrect. 'Blue' is the color of a clear sky. Snow is white.
Correct. Snow is white.
You point at a blue pen. Say what it is. Write the sentence.
'It is a blue pen.' puts the color 'blue' before the object 'pen.'
You point at a black chair. Say what it is. Write the sentence.
'It is a black chair.' puts the color 'black' before the object 'chair.'
Tap what you hear.
The audio says 'a green apple': the color 'green' goes before 'apple.'
Choose the correct question.
'What is this?' is the correct question order: what + is + this.
Correct. A question starts with 'what,' then 'is,' then 'this': What is this?
Incorrect. In a question, 'is' goes before 'this.' We say 'What is this?'
Incorrect. The question word 'what' goes first. We say 'What is this?'
Complete the question: ___ is this?
'What' asks about a thing, so the question is 'What is this?'
Incorrect. 'It' starts an answer, like 'It is a pen.' A question about a thing starts with 'what.'
Incorrect. 'An' is an article, like 'an apple.' The question needs the question word 'what.'
Correct. 'What' asks about a thing: What is this?
Your friend asks, 'What is this?' You see a pen. Choose the correct answer.
'It is a pen.' answers the question and uses 'a' before the consonant sound in 'pen.'
Incorrect. 'Pen' starts with a consonant sound, so we use 'a,' not 'an.'
Correct. 'It is a pen.' answers the question with 'a' before the consonant sound in 'pen.'
Incorrect. This answer is missing an article. We say 'It is a pen.'
Which sentence asks a question?
'What is this?' asks a question. The other sentences are statements that tell us something.
Incorrect. 'It is a bag.' tells us something. It is a statement, not a question.
Correct. 'What is this?' asks about a thing and ends with a question mark.
Incorrect. 'It is a red pen.' tells us the color and the object. It is a statement, not a question.
You do not know what an object is. Ask your friend about it. Write the question.
'What is this?' is the question we ask about an object we do not know.
Your friend asks, 'What is this?' It is a white egg. Write your answer.
'It is a white egg.' answers 'What is this?' with the color 'white' before 'egg.'
Tap what you hear.
The audio says 'what is this,' the question we ask about an object.
Food & Drink

Welcome to Food & Drink! In this unit, you learn to say what you like: I like tea. You practice questions like Do you like coffee? and short answers such as Yes, I do. You also learn breakfast words and how to order politely with I want coffee, please.
Choose the correct sentence.
With I, use the base form like. I like tea. is the correct pattern for saying what you enjoy.
Likes goes with he or she. With I, use like.
Correct. After I, use the base form like.
Me cannot be the subject of the sentence. Use I: I like tea.
Complete the sentence: I ___ coffee.
After I, use like. I like coffee. says what you enjoy.
Correct. I takes the base form like.
Likes goes with he or she, not with I.
Do not put am before like. Like works alone: I like coffee.
Which sentence says you enjoy apples?
I like apples. means you enjoy apples. Like names the foods you enjoy.
I want apples. means you would like to have them now, not that you enjoy them in general.
Am is a form of be and says what you are. This sentence does not make sense.
Correct. Like says you enjoy something.
Choose the correct negative sentence.
Use don't before like to say no. I don't like milk. is the negative form.
English does not use no before the verb. Use don't: I don't like milk.
Correct. Don't before like makes the sentence negative.
After don't, use the base form like, not likes.
Say that you like tea. Write the sentence.
I like tea. puts like right after I to say what you enjoy.
Say that you like apples. Write the sentence.
I like apples. uses like after I to name a food you enjoy.
Tap what you hear.
The phrase is I like coffee. Like tells what you enjoy.
Choose the correct question.
Yes/no questions with like start with Do: Do you like tea?
Correct. Do comes first, then you, then the base verb like.
The question cannot start with like. Start with Do: Do you like tea?
After do, use the base form like, not likes.
Complete the question: ___ you like coffee?
Use Do to start a question with like: Do you like coffee?
Are belongs to the verb be. Questions with like use Do.
Is belongs to the verb be and goes with he, she, or it. Use Do here.
Correct. Do starts a yes/no question with like.
Someone asks, "Do you like milk?" You like it. Choose the short answer.
Short answers to do-questions repeat do: Yes, I do.
A short answer ends with do, not like. Say Yes, I do.
Correct. Short answers repeat do: Yes, I do.
Am answers be-questions. This question uses do, so answer Yes, I do.
Someone asks, "Do you like eggs?" You do not like them. Choose the short answer.
The negative short answer uses don't: No, I don't.
Correct. The negative short answer is No, I don't.
Am not answers be-questions. This question uses do, so say No, I don't.
The short answer stops after don't. Do not add like.
Ask a friend if they like tea. Write the question.
Do you like tea? follows the order Do + you + like + tea.
Answer yes to the question "Do you like coffee?" Write the short answer.
Yes, I do. is the short answer. Repeat do instead of the whole sentence.
Tap what you hear.
The question is Do you like milk? It starts with Do.
Which one is a drink?
Milk is a drink. Bread and egg are foods you eat.
Bread is a food you eat, not a drink.
An egg is a food you eat, not a drink.
Correct. Milk is a drink.
Which one do you eat, not drink?
Bread is a food you eat. Water and tea are drinks.
Water is a drink, not a food you eat.
Correct. Bread is a food you eat.
Tea is a drink, not a food you eat.
Which one is a fruit?
An apple is a fruit. Milk and egg are not fruits.
Correct. An apple is a fruit.
Milk is a drink, not a fruit.
An egg is a breakfast food, but it is not a fruit.
Complete the sentence: I drink ___ in the morning.
Coffee is a drink, so it fits after drink: I drink coffee in the morning.
You eat bread. You do not drink it.
You eat an egg. You do not drink it.
Correct. Coffee is a drink.
Say that you like bread and milk. Write the sentence.
I like bread and milk. uses and to join two foods.
Say that you like eggs. Write the sentence.
I like eggs. names a breakfast food you enjoy.
Tap what you hear.
The phrase is bread and milk, two common breakfast words.
Choose the polite way to order coffee.
I want coffee, please. is polite because it ends with please.
Give me coffee. has no please and sounds rude.
Correct. Please makes the request polite.
Coffee now. sounds demanding. Use a full sentence with please.
Complete the sentence: I ___ water, please.
After I, use the base form want: I want water, please.
Correct. I takes the base form want.
Wants goes with he or she, not with I.
Do not put am before want. Want works alone: I want water.
Which word makes a request polite?
Please makes a request polite: I want tea, please.
Yes answers a question. It does not make a request polite.
No answers a question. It does not make a request polite.
Correct. Please is the polite word in a request.
Choose the correct sentence.
The correct order is I + want + tea, with please at the end.
Correct. The order is I + want + tea, with please at the end.
Wants goes with he or she. With I, use want.
The word order is wrong. Start with I: I want tea, please.
Order a coffee politely. Write the sentence.
I want coffee, please. uses want and please to order politely.
Ask for water politely. Write the sentence.
I want water, please. is a polite request ending with please.
Tap what you hear.
The sentence is I want tea, please. Please makes it polite.
Days & Time

In this unit, you learn the seven days of the week, from Monday to Sunday. You practice the small words 'today,' 'tomorrow,' and 'yesterday' to say when things happen. You also ask and answer a very useful question: 'What day is it?' Finally, you greet people in the morning, the afternoon, and the evening.
Which day comes after Monday?
Tuesday is the day right after Monday in the week.
Sunday comes before Monday, not after it.
Correct! Tuesday is the day right after Monday.
Friday comes four days after Monday, not right after it.
Which day comes after Friday?
Saturday is the day right after Friday.
Correct! Saturday is the day right after Friday.
Monday comes after the weekend; it does not follow Friday directly.
Wednesday comes before Friday, in the middle of the week.
Which day comes before Sunday?
Saturday is the day right before Sunday.
Monday comes after Sunday, not before it.
Thursday is three days before Sunday, not the day right before it.
Correct! Saturday is the day right before Sunday.
How many days are in a week?
A week has seven days, from Monday to Sunday.
Five is the number of weekdays from Monday to Friday, but a full week has seven days.
Correct! A week has seven days.
Ten is too many; a full week has seven days.
Tell a friend that today is Monday. Write the sentence.
Use 'Today is' plus the day: Today is Monday.
Say that the week has seven days. Write the sentence.
The sentence 'The week has seven days' tells how many days a week has.
Tap what you hear.
The audio says 'Today is Monday.'
Today is Monday. What day is tomorrow?
Tomorrow is the day after today, and the day after Monday is Tuesday.
Correct! Tomorrow is the day after today, and the day after Monday is Tuesday.
Sunday is the day before Monday, so it was yesterday, not tomorrow.
Monday is today, not tomorrow.
Today is Friday. What day was yesterday?
Yesterday is the day before today, and the day before Friday is Thursday.
Saturday is the day after Friday, so it is tomorrow, not yesterday.
Sunday comes two days after Friday; it was not yesterday.
Correct! Yesterday is the day before today, and the day before Friday is Thursday.
Which word means the day we are in right now?
'Today' names the day happening right now.
Correct! 'Today' means this day, the one happening now.
'Tomorrow' means the day after today.
'Yesterday' means the day before today.
Complete the sentence: ___ is Tuesday, so tomorrow is Wednesday.
If today is Tuesday, tomorrow is the next day, Wednesday.
If yesterday was Tuesday, tomorrow would be Thursday, not Wednesday.
Correct! If today is Tuesday, then tomorrow is the next day, Wednesday.
'Tomorrow' cannot be both Tuesday and Wednesday; the sentence needs 'Today.'
Tell a friend that tomorrow is Tuesday. Write the sentence.
Use 'Tomorrow is' plus the day: Tomorrow is Tuesday.
Say that yesterday was Sunday. Write the sentence.
Yesterday is in the past, so use 'was': Yesterday was Sunday.
Tap what you hear.
The audio says 'Tomorrow is Tuesday.'
Choose the question that asks about the day.
'What day is it?' is the question people use to ask about the day.
Correct! 'What day is it?' asks about the day of the week.
'How are you?' asks how a person feels, not what day it is.
'What is your name?' asks for a name, not the day.
Someone asks, 'What day is it?' Choose the best answer.
The question asks about the day, so the answer names a day: 'It is Friday.'
'I am fine' answers 'How are you?', not a question about the day.
'My name is Anna' answers a question about a name.
Correct! 'It is Friday' names the day, so it answers the question.
Complete the question: What ___ is it today?
The word 'day' completes the question: What day is it today?
'What name is it today?' is not a natural question; 'name' is used for people and things.
Correct! 'What day is it today?' asks for the day of the week.
We ask about the day, not the week, in this question; 'week' means seven days together.
Choose the correct word order for the question.
English questions start with the question word: What day is it?
'Day what is it?' has the wrong word order; the question starts with 'What.'
'Is it what day?' is not correct; 'What day' goes at the start of the question.
Correct! 'What day is it?' is the correct word order for this question.
Ask a friend what day it is today. Write the question.
Start with 'What day' and end with a question mark: What day is it today?
A friend asks, 'What day is it?' Answer that it is Friday. Write the sentence.
Answer with 'It is' plus the day: It is Friday.
Tap what you hear.
The audio asks 'What day is it?'
Which part of the day comes first?
The day starts with the morning, then comes the afternoon, then the evening.
The evening comes at the end of the day, not the start.
Correct! The morning is the first part of the day.
The afternoon comes after the morning, in the middle of the day.
Choose the greeting for the morning.
'Good morning' is the greeting people use in the morning.
Correct! 'Good morning' is the greeting for the first part of the day.
'Good night' is for saying goodbye at bedtime, not for greeting someone in the morning.
'Good evening' is a greeting for the end of the day.
Which part of the day comes between the morning and the evening?
The afternoon sits between the morning and the evening.
The night comes after the evening, at the end of the day.
A week is seven days; it is not a part of one day.
Correct! The afternoon comes after the morning and before the evening.
It is late, and you are going to bed. What do you say?
'Good night' is what people say before going to bed.
Correct! People say 'Good night' before they go to bed.
'Good afternoon' is a greeting for the middle of the day.
'Good morning' is a greeting for the start of the day.
Greet your friend Anna in the morning. Write the greeting.
Use 'Good morning' plus the name: Good morning, Anna.
Greet someone in the evening. Write the greeting.
'Good evening' is the greeting for the end of the day.
Tap what you hear.
The audio says 'Good afternoon.'

passage.video_prompt: A warm, friendly 2D-animated morning at a small school: a girl named Anna arrives, waves, and says 'Good morning.' to her teacher; a boy asks her name; everyone smiles. Child-friendly, bright colours, slow pacing; each spoken line appears as an on-screen subtitle.
